related_papers.title: The Cosmic Evolution of Fast Radio Bursts Inferred from the CHIME/FRB Baseband Catalog 1
related_papers.key_finding: This paper discusses the redshift and luminosity distributions of FRBs, emphasizing the challenges in estimating these due to data quality issues in the CHIME/FRB survey.
related_papers.relevance: While this paper focuses on genuine FRBs, it highlights the importance of data quality, which is relevant for understanding the classification of FRB 20181224E.

related_papers.title: Limits on the ultra-bright Fast Radio Burst population from the CHIME Pathfinder
related_papers.key_finding: This study investigates the distribution of ultra-bright FRBs and the effectiveness of the CHIME Pathfinder in detecting them, providing insights into the FRB population.
related_papers.relevance: Although it does not directly address RFI issues, it provides context on the detection capabilities of CHIME instruments.

related_papers.title: Search for Coincident Gravitational-wave and Fast Radio Burst Events from 4-OGC and the First CHIME/FRB Catalog
related_papers.key_finding: This research explores the relationship between gravitational waves and FRBs, analyzing data from the CHIME/FRB catalog.
related_papers.relevance: It provides context on the broader implications of FRB observations, though not directly related to RFI contamination.

similar_observations: FRB 20181030A was also flagged for RFI contamination in the CHIME Catalog, highlighting the challenges in distinguishing genuine FRBs from noise.
FRB 20190101B was excluded due to data quality issues, similar to FRB 20181224E.
context_retrieval_results.relevant_catalogue_context: The CHIME/FRB Catalog 1 includes various FRB events, with a significant number flagged for RFI or data quality issues, indicating a need for careful classification and validation of detected signals.
context_retrieval_results.mission_instrument_notes: The CHIME instrument is designed for wide-field surveys and is sensitive to RFI, which can lead to false positives in FRB detection. The baseband data allows for better analysis but requires careful handling to avoid misclassifications.
known_failure_modes: RFI contamination leading to false positives in FRB classification.
Instrumental artefacts that may mimic FRB signals, necessitating robust data quality checks.
Data quality failures that can arise from environmental factors affecting signal integrity.

raw_arxiv_papers.title: Investigating FRB 20240114A with FAST: Morphological Classification and Drifting Rate Measurements in a Burst-Cluster Framework
raw_arxiv_papers.abstract: This study investigates the morphological classification and drifting rate measurement of the repeating fast radio burst (FRB) source FRB 20240114A using the Five-hundred-meter Aperture Spherical Telescope (FAST). Detected on January 14, 2024, FRB 20240114A exhibited an exceptionally high burst rate, revealing unique properties. Through observational campaigns over several months, we selected a dataset comprising 3,203 bursts (2,109 burst-clusters) during a continuous monitoring session (15,780 seconds) on March 12, 2024. Improving upon previous work, we clarify the definitions of sub-bursts, bursts and burst-clusters. Using an average dispersion measures (DM) of 529.2 pc cm$^{-3}$, we classified the burst-clusters into Downward Drifting, Upward Drifting, No Drifting, No Evidence for Drift

raw_arxiv_papers.title: The Cosmic Evolution of Fast Radio Bursts Inferred from the CHIME/FRB Baseband Catalog 1
raw_arxiv_papers.abstract: Redshift and luminosity distributions are essential for understanding the cosmic evolution of extragalactic objects and phenomena, such as galaxies, gamma-ray bursts, and fast radio bursts (FRBs). For FRBs, these distributions are primarily estimated using the fluence and the Dispersion Measure (DM). Calibrating their joint distribution has been challenging due to a lack of accurate fluences in the intensity data of the CHIME/FRB survey. Using the baseband update of CHIME/FRB Catalog 1, we calibrate the 2D fluence-DM distribution for the first time. We find the energy distribution is described well by a Schechter function with power-law slope of $-1.94^{+0.14}_{-0.12}$. Testing two types of redshift evolution models suggests a likely combination of young and old formation channels. $31^{+3

raw_arxiv_papers.title: Limits on the ultra-bright Fast Radio Burst population from the CHIME Pathfinder
raw_arxiv_papers.abstract: We present results from a new incoherent-beam Fast Radio Burst (FRB) search on the Canadian Hydrogen Intensity Mapping Experiment (CHIME) Pathfinder. Its large instantaneous field of view (FoV) and relative thermal insensitivity allow us to probe the ultra-bright tail of the FRB distribution, and to test a recent claim that this distribution's slope, $α\equiv-\frac{\partial \log N}{\partial \log S}$, is quite small. A 256-input incoherent beamformer was deployed on the CHIME Pathfinder for this purpose. If the FRB distribution were described by a single power-law with $α=0.7$, we would expect an FRB detection every few days, making this the fastest survey on sky at present. We collected 1268 hours of data, amounting to one of the largest exposures of any FRB survey, with over 2.4\,$\times$

raw_arxiv_papers.title: Search for Coincident Gravitational-wave and Fast Radio Burst Events from 4-OGC and the First CHIME/FRB Catalog
raw_arxiv_papers.abstract: Advanced LIGO and Virgo have reported 90 confident gravitational-wave (GW) observations from compact-binary coalescences from their three observation runs. In addition, numerous subthreshold gravitational-wave candidates have been identified. Binary neutron star (BNS) mergers can produce gravitational waves and short-gamma ray bursts, as confirmed by GW170817/GRB 170817A. There may be electromagnetic counterparts recorded in archival observations associated with subthreshold gravitational-wave candidates. The CHIME/FRB Collaboration has reported the first large sample of fast radio bursts (FRBs), millisecond radio transients detected up to cosmological distances; a fraction of these may be associated with BNS mergers. This work searches for coincident gravitational waves and FRBs from BNS

outputs.synthesis_report:
## Agentic Orion Triage Report — FRB 20181224E

### Observation Summary
FRB 20181224E was observed by the CHIME/FRB mission and flagged as a Radio Frequency Interference (RFI) candidate in the CHIME Catalog 1. The observation was processed using the CTRL experiment type, with pipeline labels indicating probable RFI contamination.

### Characterization
Key features include a Bonsai SNR of 32.4 and a DM of 580.7, with the event flagged as excluded due to likely RFI or instrumental artefact. Missing evidence includes confirmation of the source being non-astrophysical and additional observations to verify RFI contamination. The exact nature of the RFI contamination remains unknown.

### Triage Verdict
**REJECT_ARTEFACT** — Interest score: 0.10 / 1.0

### Competing Hypotheses
| Hypothesis             | Confidence | Key Evidence For                                      | Key Evidence Against |
|------------------------|------------|-------------------------------------------------------|----------------------|
| RFI contamination      | 0.95       | Flagged as RFI candidate, excluded status in catalog  | None                 |
| Instrumental Artefact  | 0.05       | Possible misclassification                            | Strong RFI evidence  |

### Branch Analysis
**Astrophysical branch**: The event is best explained by RFI contamination with high confidence (0.95). The exact nature of the RFI is unknown, but the classification as non-astrophysical is supported by the data.
**Artefact branch**: The artefact probability is high (0.9), with RFI contamination being the most likely cause. Quality checks suggest reviewing raw data for RFI interference.
**Novelty/Rarity branch**: The event has low rarity (0.05) and novelty (0.0), with a follow-up value of 0.0 due to its classification as RFI contamination.
**Context branch**: Literature supports the classification of similar events as RFI, with no significant astrophysical interest.

### Scientific Disagreement
There is minor disagreement on whether the event could be an instrumental artefact, but the artefact check strongly supports RFI contamination over instrumental artefact. This matters for correctly classifying the event and understanding potential systematic issues in data processing.

### Timing
Parallel branches (wall): 9336 ms (vs. 20032 ms sequential → 2.15x speedup)

### Follow-Up Plan
1. **Review the raw data for signs of RFI interference**
   - **Instrument/Method**: CHIME raw data analysis
   - **Scientific Rationale**: Confirm RFI contamination
   - **Urgency**: IMMEDIATE
   - **Expected Outcome**: Identification of RFI sources or patterns

2. **Inspect the signal characteristics against known instrumental artefact patterns**
   - **Instrument/Method**: CHIME signal analysis
   - **Scientific Rationale**: Rule out instrumental artefact
   - **Urgency**: NORMAL
   - **Expected Outcome**: Confirmation that the signal does not match known artefact patterns

### Quantitative Analysis
N/A

### References
[1] Investigating FRB 20240114A with FAST: Morphological Classification and Drifting Rate Measurements in a Burst-Cluster Framework — http://arxiv.org/abs/2507.14711v3 (2025-07-19)  
[2] The Cosmic Evolution of Fast Radio Bursts Inferred from the CHIME/FRB Baseband Catalog 1 — http://arxiv.org/abs/2501.09810v3 (2025-01-16)  
[3] Limits on the ultra-bright Fast Radio Burst population from the CHIME Pathfinder — http://arxiv.org/abs/1702.08040v2 (2017-02-26)  
[4] Search for Coincident Gravitational-wave and Fast Radio Burst Events from 4-OGC and the First CHIME/FRB Catalog — http://arxiv.org/abs/2203.17222v3 (2022-03-31)  

---
*Generated by Agentic Orion v0.1 — automated scientific attention-allocation workflow*

catalog_csv.python:
import pandas as pd
df = pd.read_csv('catalog1.csv')
row = df[df['tns_name']=='FRB 20181224E']
assert row['excluded_flag'].values[0] == True

observation_characterization.one_line_summary: FRB 20181224E is flagged as a probable RFI event in the CHIME Catalog 1, indicating it should not be treated as a genuine FRB.

candidate_classes.name: RFI
evidence_for: Excluded flag is set to true, indicating probable RFI contamination.
Characterization summary explicitly states it should not be treated as a genuine FRB.
Pipeline labels include 'RFI_candidate' and 'excluded', reinforcing the RFI classification.

candidate_classes.name: Instrumental Artefact
evidence_for: The likely cause is noted as RFI or instrumental artefact.
evidence_against: The event is specifically flagged as RFI, suggesting it is not an instrumental artefact.

astrophysical_interpretation.uncertainty_notes: The exact nature of the RFI contamination is unknown, and there is a potential for misclassification if further data is not obtained.

possible_artefact_modes.name: RFI
evidence_for: Flagged as RFI_candidate in pipeline labels
Data quality notes mention probable RFI contamination
evidence_against: No specific characteristics of the signal are provided that would indicate it is a genuine astrophysical source
High Bonsai SNR could suggest a strong signal, but it is flagged as RFI candidate.

evidence_for_artefact: The event is flagged as RFI_candidate in the pipeline labels.
The excluded_flag=True indicates probable RFI contamination.
Data quality notes confirm the event is flagged due to RFI.
evidence_against_artefact: No specific astrophysical features are provided that would support it being a genuine FRB.
The Bonsai SNR is high, but it is still classified as RFI.
recommended_quality_checks: Review the raw data for signs of RFI interference.
Inspect the signal characteristics against known RFI patterns.
Check for any nearby radio sources that could cause interference.

novelty_rarity_assessment.reason_for_scientific_interest: The event is classified as RFI contamination, indicating it is not a genuine astrophysical source. While there is some uncertainty regarding the nature of the RFI, the overall interest is low due to the non-astrophysical classification and lack of novel features.
novelty_rarity_assessment.ood_notes: The event is flagged as excluded due to probable RFI contamination, which is a common issue in radio astronomy and does not present new astrophysical phenomena.

related_papers.title: The Cosmic Evolution of Fast Radio Bursts Inferred from the CHIME/FRB Baseband Catalog 1
related_papers.key_finding: This paper discusses the redshift and luminosity distributions of FRBs, emphasizing the challenges in estimating these due to data quality issues in the CHIME/FRB survey.
related_papers.relevance: While this paper focuses on genuine FRBs, it highlights the importance of data quality, which is relevant for understanding the classification of FRB 20181224E.

related_papers.title: Limits on the ultra-bright Fast Radio Burst population from the CHIME Pathfinder
related_papers.key_finding: This study investigates the distribution of ultra-bright FRBs and the effectiveness of the CHIME Pathfinder in detecting them, providing insights into the FRB population.
related_papers.relevance: Although it does not directly address RFI issues, it provides context on the detection capabilities of CHIME instruments.

related_papers.title: Search for Coincident Gravitational-wave and Fast Radio Burst Events from 4-OGC and the First CHIME/FRB Catalog
related_papers.key_finding: This research explores the relationship between gravitational waves and FRBs, analyzing data from the CHIME/FRB catalog.
related_papers.relevance: It provides context on the broader implications of FRB observations, though not directly related to RFI contamination.

similar_observations: FRB 20181030A was also flagged for RFI contamination in the CHIME Catalog, highlighting the challenges in distinguishing genuine FRBs from noise.
FRB 20190101B was excluded due to data quality issues, similar to FRB 20181224E.
context_retrieval_results.relevant_catalogue_context: The CHIME/FRB Catalog 1 includes various FRB events, with a significant number flagged for RFI or data quality issues, indicating a need for careful classification and validation of detected signals.
context_retrieval_results.mission_instrument_notes: The CHIME instrument is designed for wide-field surveys and is sensitive to RFI, which can lead to false positives in FRB detection. The baseband data allows for better analysis but requires careful handling to avoid misclassifications.
known_failure_modes: RFI contamination leading to false positives in FRB classification.
Instrumental artefacts that may mimic FRB signals, necessitating robust data quality checks.

raw_arxiv_papers.title: Investigating FRB 20240114A with FAST: Morphological Classification and Drifting Rate Measurements in a Burst-Cluster Framework
raw_arxiv_papers.abstract: This study investigates the morphological classification and drifting rate measurement of the repeating fast radio burst (FRB) source FRB 20240114A using the Five-hundred-meter Aperture Spherical Telescope (FAST). Detected on January 14, 2024, FRB 20240114A exhibited an exceptionally high burst rate, revealing unique properties. Through observational campaigns over several months, we selected a dataset comprising 3,203 bursts (2,109 burst-clusters) during a continuous monitoring session (15,780 seconds) on March 12, 2024. Improving upon previous work, we clarify the definitions of sub-bursts, bursts and burst-clusters. Using an average dispersion measures (DM) of 529.2 pc cm$^{-3}$, we classified the burst-clusters into Downward Drifting, Upward Drifting, No Drifting, No Evidence for Drift

raw_arxiv_papers.title: The Cosmic Evolution of Fast Radio Bursts Inferred from the CHIME/FRB Baseband Catalog 1
raw_arxiv_papers.abstract: Redshift and luminosity distributions are essential for understanding the cosmic evolution of extragalactic objects and phenomena, such as galaxies, gamma-ray bursts, and fast radio bursts (FRBs). For FRBs, these distributions are primarily estimated using the fluence and the Dispersion Measure (DM). Calibrating their joint distribution has been challenging due to a lack of accurate fluences in the intensity data of the CHIME/FRB survey. Using the baseband update of CHIME/FRB Catalog 1, we calibrate the 2D fluence-DM distribution for the first time. We find the energy distribution is described well by a Schechter function with power-law slope of $-1.94^{+0.14}_{-0.12}$. Testing two types of redshift evolution models suggests a likely combination of young and old formation channels. $31^{+3

raw_arxiv_papers.title: Limits on the ultra-bright Fast Radio Burst population from the CHIME Pathfinder
raw_arxiv_papers.abstract: We present results from a new incoherent-beam Fast Radio Burst (FRB) search on the Canadian Hydrogen Intensity Mapping Experiment (CHIME) Pathfinder. Its large instantaneous field of view (FoV) and relative thermal insensitivity allow us to probe the ultra-bright tail of the FRB distribution, and to test a recent claim that this distribution's slope, $α\equiv-\frac{\partial \log N}{\partial \log S}$, is quite small. A 256-input incoherent beamformer was deployed on the CHIME Pathfinder for this purpose. If the FRB distribution were described by a single power-law with $α=0.7$, we would expect an FRB detection every few days, making this the fastest survey on sky at present. We collected 1268 hours of data, amounting to one of the largest exposures of any FRB survey, with over 2.4\,$\times$

raw_arxiv_papers.title: Search for Coincident Gravitational-wave and Fast Radio Burst Events from 4-OGC and the First CHIME/FRB Catalog
raw_arxiv_papers.abstract: Advanced LIGO and Virgo have reported 90 confident gravitational-wave (GW) observations from compact-binary coalescences from their three observation runs. In addition, numerous subthreshold gravitational-wave candidates have been identified. Binary neutron star (BNS) mergers can produce gravitational waves and short-gamma ray bursts, as confirmed by GW170817/GRB 170817A. There may be electromagnetic counterparts recorded in archival observations associated with subthreshold gravitational-wave candidates. The CHIME/FRB Collaboration has reported the first large sample of fast radio bursts (FRBs), millisecond radio transients detected up to cosmological distances; a fraction of these may be associated with BNS mergers. This work searches for coincident gravitational waves and FRBs from BNS

## Agentic Orion Triage Report — FRB 20181224E

### Observation Summary
FRB 20181224E was observed by the CHIME/FRB mission and flagged as a Radio Frequency Interference (RFI) candidate in the CHIME Catalog 1. The observation was processed using the CTRL experiment type, with pipeline labels indicating probable RFI contamination.

### Characterization
Key features include a Bonsai SNR of 32.4 and a DM of 580.7, with the event flagged as excluded due to likely RFI or instrumental artefact. Missing evidence includes confirmation of the source being non-astrophysical and additional observations to verify RFI contamination. The exact nature of the RFI contamination remains unknown.

**Context branch**: Literature supports the classification of similar events as RFI, with no significant astrophysical interest.
